question: Explain the key differences between asymmetric multiprocessing (ASMP) and symmetric multiprocessing (SMP) in terms of processor scheduling responsibilities.
expectedPoints: In ASMP, a single master processor handles all scheduling decisions, I/O processing, and other system activities, while the other processors (slaves) execute only user code.
In SMP, all processors are peers; each processor is self-scheduling, obtaining its next task from a shared ready queue.
SMP is more common in modern operating systems as it avoids the bottleneck of a single master processor and allows for better load balancing.

question: Define processor affinity and describe why it is an important consideration in multiple-processor scheduling.
expectedPoints: Processor affinity is a policy where a process has a preference to continue executing on the same processor it was previously assigned.
It is important because migrating a process to a different processor invalidates the data in the first processor's cache (cache miss), which can be a costly operation.
This concept leads to two types: soft affinity (an attempt by the scheduler to keep a process on one CPU) and hard affinity (allowing a process to specify a set of CPUs it may run on).

question: Compare and contrast the Gang Scheduling algorithm with the Dedicated Processor Assignment approach. Discuss a scenario where one would be significantly more advantageous than the other.
expectedPoints: Gang Scheduling schedules all threads of a process (a gang) to run simultaneously on different processors, facilitating efficient communication and synchronization by preventing blocking.
Dedicated Processor Assignment assigns a specific set of processors to a job for the duration of its execution, treating a multiprocessor system like a collection of independent uniprocessors.
Gang Scheduling is highly advantageous for tightly-coupled parallel applications where threads frequently synchronize (e.g., scientific computing).

Dedicated Processor Assignment can be more advantageous in a real-time system or a heavily loaded batch system where predictable performance and isolation are critical, avoiding context switching and scheduling overhead.
